range.conservationStatus: Not ranked; widely distributed — local populations vary
range.geographicRange: Eastern North America; common in northeastern deciduous forests

elevationRange.note: In Pike County, most reports below ~1,200 ft in late spring; timing shifts with elevation

ecology.role: Saprotrophic and possibly facultatively mycorrhizal — decomposes organic matter and may associate with living hardwood roots. Fruiting bodies release spores that colonize disturbed soils and dying tree roots.

associatedSpecies.name: American elm
associatedSpecies.relationship: Often fruits near dying or dead elm roots

associatedSpecies.name: Apple
associatedSpecies.relationship: Old orchard edges — historical land use signal

associatedSpecies.name: Tulip poplar
associatedSpecies.relationship: Reported on slopes with tulip poplar in Appalachians

habitat.summary: Moist, calcareous or rich soils at forest edges, river terraces, and old orchard margins — especially after soil warms following spring rain.
preferred: North-facing slopes below 1,200 ft in late spring
habitat.substrate: Leaf litter over mineral soil; often near dead or dying hardwood roots

phenology.seasonality: Spring ephemeral — fruiting body appears for days to weeks, not year-round
phenology.activePeriod: Late April through late May in northeastern Pennsylvania; earlier on warm south aspects, later above 1,200 ft

regionalNotes.pike-county-pa: May fade below 1,200 ft by late May during warm, dry spells; shaded terraces after rain remain the best teaching sites.

identification.summary: A honeycombed, hollow-stemmed spring mushroom — one of the most sought-after fungi in eastern forests, and one of the most dangerous if confused with toxic look-alikes.
fieldMarks: Cap honeycombed with pits and ridges — not brain-like folds
Cap attached to stem at base (not free-hanging skirt)
Entirely hollow from cap tip through stem when sliced lengthwise
Cream to yellow-brown color; size variable (2–12 cm cap typical)

lookAlikes.name: False morels (Gyromitra spp.)
lookAlikes.distinction: Brain-like wrinkled cap, not honeycombed; NOT reliably hollow — potentially deadly if eaten

lookAlikes.name: Verpa bohemica (early morel relative)
lookAlikes.distinction: Cap attached only at apex — cap hangs like a thimble; stem may be cottony inside

considerations: Never eat any wild mushroom without expert confirmation — false morels can be fatal
Cook all morels thoroughly; raw consumption may cause illness even in true morels
Individual sensitivity varies — start with small portions if consuming

observation: Photograph in habitat before considering harvest
Cut at soil line if harvesting legally — do not rake leaf litter
Leave small specimens for spore dispersal and teaching
Never share exact GPS coordinates of fruiting patches publicly
leaveNoTrace: Stay on durable surfaces when trails are muddy
Do not dig soil or disturb roots searching for buried sclerotia
Pack out all trash from orchard and terrace walks
ethics.conservation: Sustainable harvest means taking only what you can identify with certainty, on land where you have permission, without exhausting local patches. Education value exceeds harvest value for most learners.

tips: Include substrate, nearby tree bark, and aspect of slope in frame
Photograph cap surface and stem base — key ID features
Use diffused light; avoid flash that washes out honeycomb texture
A single in-situ photo teaches more than a basket glamour shot

education.overview: The yellow morel is a spring icon of eastern forests — a teacher of soil moisture, dying hardwood ecology, and the patience required for fungal identification.
education.summary: Learn the honeycomb, the hollow stem, and the habitat story before you ever consider taste.
interestingFacts: Morel fruiting often follows dying elm and ash — a visible signal of forest change
The same hillside may fruit in different weeks each year depending on rain and warmth
Morels are ascomycetes — their spores develop inside microscopic sacs, not on gills
fieldNotes: Ask: What hardwoods stand within ten paces?
Press leaf litter — is moisture still present two days after rain?
Compare north vs. south aspect on the same hill before concluding the season is over

relatedSpecies.relationship: Summer forest mushroom — different season and habitat

futureObservations: Document fruiting date vs. last rain event and elevation
Note associated tree species at each observation
Track whether patch returns in same general area next spring
unknowns: Precise mycorrhizal partners remain under study — tree associations are ecological patterns, not guaranteed rules
Species complex taxonomy continues to be refined in eastern North America

media.alt: Yellow morel emerging from leaf litter near a hardwood edge
media.caption: Honeycombed cap and hollow stem — field photograph placeholder

prompts: General elevation and aspect — not exact coordinates publicly

research.notes: ForageCast prediction model links via externalIds.foragecastModel
